pattern: Treating each row as the base of a histogram of consecutive ones above it reduces the problem to a largest-rectangle-in-histogram scan repeated per row.
hints: For any chosen bottom row, the largest all-ones rectangle resting on it depends only on how tall the column of ones is above each cell.
Sweep rows top to bottom, maintaining for each column the running count of consecutive ones ending at the current row, which forms a histogram.
After updating the per-column heights for a row, run the linear monotonic-stack largest-rectangle-in-histogram routine on that height array and keep the maximum area seen across all rows.

edge_inputs.description: mixed grid with a wide middle band of ones
single zero cell yields zero area
single one cell yields area one
all ones forms one big rectangle
tall thin column beats a short wide row
ones split by a row of zeros

stress.description: random 0/1 grid with a bias toward ones to create sizeable rectangles
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    rows = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 40)))
    cols = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 40)))
    return [[['1' if rng.random() < 0.7 else '0' for _ in range(cols)] for _ in range(rows)]]
stress.note: 0.7 fill makes non-trivial rectangles common while still leaving gaps
